meta.name: K6 Kessler Psychological Distress Scale Brief
meta.description: The K6 is a 6-item short-form screening scale for non-specific psychological distress. It is derived from the K10 and is widely used in population health surveys to identify individuals with serious mental illness.
meta.author: Kessler, Barker, Colpe et al. (2003)
meta.reference: Kessler RC, Barker PR, Colpe LJ, et al. Screening for serious mental illness in the general population. Arch Gen Psychiatry. 2003;60(2):184-189.
meta.instruction: The following questions ask about how you have been feeling during the past 30 days. For each question, select the option that best describes how often you had this feeling.

questions.text: During the past 30 days, about how often did you feel nervous?
During the past 30 days, about how often did you feel so nervous that nothing could calm you down?
During the past 30 days, about how often did you feel hopeless?
During the past 30 days, about how often did you feel depressed?
During the past 30 days, about how often did you feel so sad that nothing could cheer you up?
During the past 30 days, about how often did you feel worthless?

ranges.label: Low Distress
ranges.description: Your score suggests low levels of psychological distress. You are likely functioning well. This indicates that your current stress levels are manageable and you are coping well with daily challenges.
suggestions: Continue maintaining healthy habits and coping strategies
Stay connected with supportive people in your life
Practice regular self-care and stress prevention
Continue using your effective stress management strategies
Regularly check in with yourself and engage in relaxation activities

ranges.label: Moderate Distress
ranges.description: Your score indicates moderate psychological distress. You may benefit from additional support. At this level, stress may cause persistent tension, mood fluctuations, and reduced work efficiency or rest quality.
suggestions: Consider speaking with a healthcare professional about your emotional well-being
Practice stress management techniques such as mindfulness or exercise
Maintain regular sleep and daily routines
Reach out to trusted friends or family for support
Consider discussing stress management strategies with a counselor

ranges.label: Severe Distress
ranges.description: Your score indicates severe psychological distress, which may suggest serious mental illness. Professional evaluation is strongly recommended. Your stress level is elevated and may be significantly affecting your physical and mental health. Seeking support and making adjustments is recommended.
suggestions: Please seek help from a mental health professional as soon as possible
If you are in crisis, contact a crisis helpline (e.g., 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline)
Talk to someone you trust about how you are feeling
Follow through with professional treatment and support
